sanskrit_devanagari:
तानि सर्वाणि संयम्य युक्त आसीत मत्परः ।
वशे हि यस्येन्द्रियाणि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ६१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tāni sarvāṇi saṁyamya yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ |
vaśe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā || 61 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.61।। rather than ॥ ६१ ॥)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'tani sarvani samyamya yukta asita matparah vase hi yasyendriyani tasya prajna pratisthita')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.061 = MBh_06,024.061). Body text identical: 'tāni sarvāṇi saṃyamya yukta āsīta matparaḥ / vaśe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā'. GRETIL writes 'saṃyamya' and 'matparaḥ' (anusvara, no hyphen) where vedabase prints 'saṁyamya' and 'mat-paraḥ' (candrabindu, hyphen-separated compound); same Sanskrit conjuncts.

anvaya.meaning: supreme / intent on (in compound mat-paraḥ: 'intent on Me as the supreme')

translations.verbatim_quote: One who restrains his senses, keeping them under full control, and fixes his consciousness upon Me, is known as a man of steady intelligence.
They are established in perfect knowledge, who subdue their senses and keep their minds ever absorbed in Me.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: That the highest conception of yoga perfection is Kṛṣṇa consciousness is clearly explained in this verse. And unless one is Kṛṣṇa conscious it is not at all possible to control the senses. As cited above, the great sage Durvāsā Muni picked a quarrel with Mahārāja Ambarīṣa, and Durvāsā Muni unnecessarily became angry out of pride and therefore could not check his senses.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Shankara bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Samyamya, controlling, having subdued; sarvani, all; tani, of them; asita, one should remain; yuktah, concentrated; mat-parah, on Me as the supreme — he to whom I, Vasudeva, the inmost Self of all, am the supreme (parah) is mat-parah. The idea is, he should remain (concentrated) thinking, 'I am not different from Him.' Hi, for; the prajna, wisdom; tasya, of one, of the sannyasin remaining thus concentrated; yasya, whose; indriyani, organs; are vase, under control, by dint of practice.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads mat-paraḥ in non-dual terms: the practitioner takes Vasudeva (Krishna) as the inmost Self of all, treating the Supreme as not-different from oneself. The senses come under control through disciplined practice (vaśe ... abhyāsataḥ). The verse's structural claim is conjunctive: BOTH sense-restraint AND being-intent-on-Me are required; sense-restraint by itself produces only the seated form, not the wisdom established in the seated form.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Ramanuja bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: With a desire to overcome this mutual dependence between the subduing of the senses and vision of the self, one has to conquer the senses which are difficult to subdue on account of their attachment to sense-objects. So, focussing the mind on Me who am the only auspicious object for meditation, let him remain steadfast. When the mind is focussed on Me as its object, then such a mind, purified by the burning away of all impurities and devoid of attachment to the senses, is able to control the senses.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as the resolution of the mutual-dependence trap diagnosed in 2.60. The senses cannot be subdued by sheer will because the senses are attached to objects; the will, in turn, cannot reach the self while the senses are still pulled outward. Ramanuja's solution: fix the mind on Krishna as the only auspicious object of meditation. Once the mind is so focussed, it is purified of impurities and detached from the senses, and the senses can then be controlled. Sense-control issues from the meditative focus, not from direct combat with the senses.

positions.rendering: fixes his consciousness upon Me (in relationship with Me)
keep their minds ever absorbed in Me
on Me as the supreme — taking Vasudeva, the inmost Self of all, as not-different from oneself (Advaita reading)
focussing the mind on Me who am the only auspicious object for meditation (Vishishtadvaita reading: relational fixation on the Supreme Person)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: mat-paraḥ ('having Me as the supreme / paramount / object') is the verse's most theistically explicit term in Chapter 2 so far. The four traditions diverge sharply on what it means. Shankara reads it non-dually: the Supreme is the inmost Self, and 'fixing on Me' is recognising non-difference between the practitioner's self and Vasudeva. Ramanuja reads it relationally: Krishna is the auspicious object of meditation distinct from the practitioner; the mind fixes on him as the only worthy support for the meditative posture. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render in the modern-devotional register that is acceptable across traditions but lexically closer to Ramanuja's reading. The lexical underdetermination is real; the verse text alone does not adjudicate.

positions.rendering: is known as a man of steady intelligence (folds yuktaḥ into the result, not the precondition)
should remain concentrated (āsīta = should sit/remain; yuktaḥ = concentrated)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: āsīta is an optative ('should sit / should remain'); yuktaḥ ('yoked, joined, disciplined') is the manner of the sitting. The verse prescribes a posture, not a result: the practitioner is told to sit/remain in the yoked state, intent on the supreme. Prabhupada's translation collapses the prescription into a description of the result (the steady-intelligence person), which is acceptable but loses the imperative force. Shankara's rendering preserves the imperative most explicitly.

literal_meaning: Having restrained all those senses, one should sit yoked, intent on Me as the supreme; for one whose senses are under control, his wisdom is established.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse is the active prescription that follows the diagnosis in 2.60. Sense-restraint alone is insufficient; it must be conjoined with the meditative focus on the Supreme. The two are not parallel disciplines — they are mutually enabling. Shankara reads the focus non-dually (the Supreme is the inmost Self); Ramanuja reads it relationally (the Supreme is the auspicious object of meditation, distinct from the practitioner); Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds it into devotional consciousness of Krishna. All traditions agree: the wisdom established (prajñā pratiṣṭhitā) is the joint product of sense-control and the directed meditative posture. The verse rejects two adjacent failures: sense-control without focus (mere asceticism that produces no wisdom) and focus without sense-control (devotion that the senses still pull off-center).

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable.
